abstract:
Absract:

The kinetics of the simplest Criegee intermediate, CH$_2$OO, were investigated in reactions with SO$_2$, NO$_2$, NO, H$_2$O, and CH$_3$CHO at 295 K and over an extended pressure range of 25–450 Torr. CH$_2$OO was generated via the flash photolysis of CH$_2$I$_2$/O$_2$ mixtures, with reaction progress monitored through the laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) detection of the HCHO product, supplemented by direct photoionisation mass spectrometry (PIMS) for the SO$_2$ system at low pressure. The rate coefficients for the reactions with SO$_2$ and NO$_2$ were determined to be $(3.42 \pm 0.42) \times 10^{-11}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$ and $(1.5 \pm 0.5) \times 10^{-12}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$, respectively. Both values remained independent of pressure across the studied ranges, suggesting that these species act as efficient atmospheric sinks for CH$_2$OO. 

In contrast, the reaction with CH$_3$CHO exhibited distinct pressure dependence; while the rate coefficient increased with pressure, the yield of HCHO decreased, indicating the collisional stabilization of a secondary ozonide at higher pressures. For the reactions with NO and H$_2$O, only upper limits could be established at $2 \times 10^{-13}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$ and $9 \times 10^{-17}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$, respectively. Notably, the upper limit for the CH$_2$OO + H$_2$O reaction is significantly lower than previously reported values. These findings suggest that the atmospheric impact of Criegee intermediates on SO$_2$ oxidation and sulfate aerosol formation may be more pronounced than currently estimated in many models, due to the diminished competitive role of water vapor.
